model_calls.raw: {"claims": [{"text": "Trunk-based development is a branching model where developers collaborate on a single branch called trunk.", "evidence_spans": [{"page": "trunk-based.md", "text": "Trunk-based development is a branching model where developers collaborate on a single branch called trunk."}]}, {"text": "Developers commit small changes to trunk at least once a day to avoid long-lived divergent branches.", "evidence_spans": [{"page": "trunk-based.md", "text": "Developers commit small changes to trunk at least once a day to avoid long-lived divergent branches."}]}, {"text": "Short-lived feature branches are permitted but should live for no more than a couple of days before being merged.", "evidence_spans": [{"page": "trunk-based.md", "text": "Short-lived feature branches are permitted but should live for no more than a couple of days."}]}, {"text": "Long-lived branches are discouraged because they cause painful merge conflicts and delay integration.", "evidence_spans": [{"page": "trunk-based.md", "text": "Long-lived branches are discouraged because they cause painful merge conflicts and delay integration."}]}, {"text": "Trunk-based development pairs well with feature flags, which let teams merge incomplete work to trunk while keeping it hidden from users until it is ready.", "evidence_spans": [{"page": "trunk-based.md", "text": "Trunk-based development pairs well with feature flags, which let teams merge incomplete work to trunk while keeping it hidden from users until it is ready."}]}]}

claims.text: Trunk-based development is a branching model where developers collaborate on a single branch called trunk.

evidence_spans.text: Trunk-based development is a branching model where developers collaborate on a single branch called trunk.

claims.text: Developers commit small changes to trunk at least once a day to avoid long-lived divergent branches.

evidence_spans.text: Developers commit small changes to trunk at least once a day to avoid long-lived divergent branches.

claims.text: Short-lived feature branches are permitted but should live for no more than a couple of days before being merged.

evidence_spans.text: Short-lived feature branches are permitted but should live for no more than a couple of days.

claims.text: Long-lived branches are discouraged because they cause painful merge conflicts and delay integration.

evidence_spans.text: Long-lived branches are discouraged because they cause painful merge conflicts and delay integration.

claims.text: Trunk-based development pairs well with feature flags, which let teams merge incomplete work to trunk while keeping it hidden from users until it is ready.

evidence_spans.text: Trunk-based development pairs well with feature flags, which let teams merge incomplete work to trunk while keeping it hidden from users until it is ready.

rendered_text:
Trunk-based development is a branching model where developers collaborate on a single branch called trunk.
Developers commit small changes to trunk at least once a day to avoid long-lived divergent branches.
Short-lived feature branches are permitted but should live for no more than a couple of days before being merged. (partially supported)
Long-lived branches are discouraged because they cause painful merge conflicts and delay integration.
Trunk-based development pairs well with feature flags, which let teams merge incomplete work to trunk while keeping it hidden from users until it is ready.
found_facts: developers collaborate on a single branch called trunk

model_calls.raw: {"claims": [{"text": "A feature flag is a configuration switch that lets a team turn a feature on or off at runtime without deploying new code.", "evidence_spans": [ { "page": "feature-flags.md", "text": "A feature flag is a configuration switch that lets a team turn a feature on or off at runtime without deploying new code." } ], "entailment_status": null}, {"text": "Feature flags decouple deployment of code from release of functionality.", "evidence_spans": [ { "page": "feature-flags.md", "text": "Feature flags decouple deployment of code from release of functionality." } ], "entailment_status": null}, {"text": "Feature flags enable progressive delivery techniques such as canary releases and percentage-based rollouts.", "evidence_spans": [ { "page": "feature-flags.md", "text": "Feature flags enable progressive delivery techniques such as canary releases and percentage-based rollouts." } ], "entailment_status": null}, {"text": "A team can expose a new feature to one percent of users first and increase the percentage as confidence grows.", "evidence_spans": [ { "page": "feature-flags.md", "text": "A team can expose a new feature to one percent of users first and increase the percentage as confidence grows." } ], "entailment_status": null}, {"text": "Stale feature flags accumulate as technical debt and should be removed once a feature is fully rolled out.", "evidence_spans": [ { "page": "feature-flags.md", "text": "Stale feature flags accumulate as burden of code paths to test and maintain." } ], "entailment_status": null}, {"text": "Long-lived flags increase the number of code paths the team must test and maintain.", "evidence_spans": [ { "page": "feature-flags.md", "text": "Long-lived flags increase the number of code paths the team must test and maintain." } ], "entailment_status": null} ]}

claims.text: A feature flag is a configuration switch that lets a team turn a feature on or off at runtime without deploying new code.

evidence_spans.text: A feature flag is a configuration switch that lets a team turn a feature on or off at runtime without deploying new code.

claims.text: Feature flags decouple deployment of code from release of functionality.

evidence_spans.text: Feature flags decouple deployment of code from release of functionality.

claims.text: Feature flags enable progressive delivery techniques such as canary releases and percentage-based rollouts.

evidence_spans.text: Feature flags enable progressive delivery techniques such as canary releases and percentage-based rollouts.

claims.text: A team can expose a new feature to one percent of users first and increase the percentage as confidence grows.

evidence_spans.text: A team can expose a new feature to one percent of users first and increase the percentage as confidence grows.

claims.text: Stale feature flags accumulate as technical debt and should be removed once a feature is fully rolled out.

evidence_spans.text: Stale feature flags accumulate as burden of code paths to test and maintain.

claims.text: Long-lived flags increase the number of code paths the team must test and maintain.

evidence_spans.text: Long-lived flags increase the number of code paths the team must test and maintain.

rendered_text:
A feature flag is a configuration switch that lets a team turn a feature on or off at runtime without deploying new code.
Feature flags decouple deployment of code from release of functionality.
Feature flags enable progressive delivery techniques such as canary releases and percentage-based rollouts.
A team can expose a new feature to one percent of users first and increase the percentage as confidence grows.
Stale feature flags accumulate as technical debt and should be removed once a feature is fully rolled out. (partially supported)
Long-lived flags increase the number of code paths the team must test and maintain.

claim_text: Elite teams deploy multiple times per day.
For elite teams the lead time for changes is under an hour.
Continuous integration means merging working copies to a shared mainline several times a day.
In TDD the failing test is written before the implementation code.
The three pillars of observability are metrics, logs, and traces.
The incident commander coordinates the response during an active incident.
A blue-green deployment runs two identical production environments called blue and green.
The assured method provides bidirectional traceability between requirements, design, and tests.
The technical debt metaphor was coined by Ward Cunningham.
Pull requests under about four hundred lines help reviewers find more defects.
Elite teams restore service in well under sixty minutes after an outage.
Code coverage reports the proportion of source lines that the tests run.
Feature flags let teams release functionality separately from deploying code.
The change failure rate for the best teams sits at roughly fifteen percent.
Trunk-based development asks developers to commit small changes daily.
Elite teams achieve a deployment frequency of exactly forty-two per hour.
The test pyramid was invented in nineteen ninety-two by Kent Beck.
Pair programming requires three developers at a single keyboard.
Blue-green deployment was mandated by the ISO 9001 standard.
Semantic version numbers contain five separate components.
The solo method forbids using any version control system at all.
Observability guarantees one hundred percent uptime for every service.
